Marcos

### Post by erginemr on 2008-02-05
Hello,

1. What is the outcome of the console command:
```
glxinfo | grep -i direct
```

2. What is the outcome of the console command:
```
compiz --replace
```

3. Which type of monitor are you using? Are you happy with the 1440x1440 resolution? Or, to put in in another way, what is your currrent resolution?

In the mean time, I will compare your xorg.conf file with mine...

---

```
# xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf manual page.

# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg

#

	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet

	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"

    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "tr"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "stylus"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "stylus"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "eraser"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "eraser"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "cursor"
    Driver         "wacom"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "SyncMaster"
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "nVidia Corporation NV17 [GeForce4 MX 440]"

Section "Screen"

    DefaultDepth    24

    Option         "NoLogo" "True"

        Depth       24
        Modes      "1024x768_75" "800x600_75" "640x480_75"
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```

### Post by kesman on 2008-02-06
Try reinstalling the driver with envy,  [http://www.albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html](http://www.albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html)
then you might be able to get your compiz work. For both of you above, you should at least comment out the wacom tablet-pc entries from your xorg.conf, since it produces error messages to the log if you don't really have a tablet and it tries to load those elements.

---

### Post by erginemr on 2008-02-06
> **kesman said:**
> Try reinstalling the driver with envy,  [http://www.albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html](http://www.albertomilone.com/nvidia_scripts1.html)
then you might be able to get your compiz work. For both of you above, you should at least comment out the wacom tablet-pc entries from your xorg.conf, since it produces error messages to the log if you don't really have a tablet and it tries to load those elements.

Thank you very much **kesman**. I will see to it. ;)

```
	# Uncomment if you have a wacom tablet
	#	InputDevice     "stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	#	InputDevice     "eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
```

should take care of the situation, shouldn't they? There is no message reported with reference to "wacom" in my "/var/log/Xorg.0.log" file. 

And neither 
```
cat /var/log/Xorg.0.log | grep -i wacom
```
nor
```
cat /var/log/dmesg | grep -i wacom
```
yielded anything. What do you think?

---

### Post by kesman on 2008-02-06
Yeah well I didn't notice those lines earlier :P
Anyhow, I like to keep my config files as minimal as possible, and extra lines in them takes longer time for the boot-up process to read trough them.(Not even a second, but still =D )

Try cat /var/log/Xorg.0.log | grep EE and grep WW to see any errors or warnings.

---

### Post by erginemr on 2008-02-06
Got it, thanks. ):P

Regarding markinf's problem, I suggest him to uninstall the binary driver (i.e. NVIDIA-*.run) first, and use Envy as you suggested, or use the binary drivers "nvidia-glx-*" provided by Synaptic, or use "restricted-manager" for an auto install.

To completely uninstall the binary driver, one should use the "--uninstall" command:
```
./NVIDIA-*.run --uninstall
```

---

### Post by erginemr on 2008-02-06
C'mon! Are you going to go back to Windoze just because Compiz does not work? Many people (including me) have deliberately disabled it, because it was futile (unnecessary) beyond some initial eye candy. 

Taking the decision to install Linux was a big step, which means you have decided to "free" yourself from the neverending cycle of "M$ crappy software -> More hardware -> More money -> Next M$ crappy Software" 

Besides, having a secondary operating system is like visiting a foreign country. As in the first, you gain a more global vision of the world, having a different perspective on the mundane events; in the second, you gain a different perspective of how things can be done differently in the virtual IT world.

So, please do not give up on your well-taken decision just for the sake of useless eye candy. At least, dual boot Ubuntu with Vista, so that you may keep on experiencing Linux.

---
